metadata.title: Exposure-Response Relationships: Study Design, Data Analysis, and Regulatory Applications

rules.rule_text: Sponsors of INDs and applicants submitting NDAs or BLAs must use exposure-response information in the development of drugs, including therapeutic biologics, as described in this guidance.
rules.source_section: Section I – Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: This document provides recommendations for sponsors of investigational new drugs (INDs) and applicants submitting new drug applications (NDAs) or biologics license applications (BLAs) on the use of exposure-response information in the development of drugs, including therapeutic biologics.

rules.rule_text: A drug can only be determined to be safe and effective when the relationship of beneficial and adverse effects to a defined exposure is known; exposure-response information is at the heart of any determination of safety and effectiveness.
rules.source_section: Section II – Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response information is at the heart of any determination of the safety and effectiveness of drugs. That is, a drug can be determined to be safe and effective only when the relationship of beneficial and adverse effects to a defined exposure is known.

rules.rule_text: Critical exposure-response studies must prospectively define hypotheses/objectives, use an appropriate control group, use randomization to ensure comparability of treatment groups and minimize bias, use well-defined and reliable methods for assessing response variables, and use other techniques to minimize bias.
rules.source_section: Section III.B – Information to Support a Determination of Safety and Efficacy
rules.verbatim_quote: Thus, we recommend that critical studies (1) have prospectively defined hypotheses/objectives, (2) use an appropriate control group, (3) use randomization to ensure comparability of treatment groups and to minimize bias, (4) use well-defined and reliable methods for assessing response variables, and (5) use other techniques to minimize bias.

rules.rule_text: A biomarker is not considered an acceptable surrogate endpoint for a determination of efficacy unless it has been empirically shown to function as a valid indicator of clinical benefit; theoretical justification alone does not meet the evidentiary standards for market access.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.1 – Biomarkers
rules.verbatim_quote: From a regulatory perspective, a biomarker is not considered an acceptable surrogate endpoint for a determination of efficacy of a new drug unless it has been empirically shown to function as a valid indicator of clinical benefit (i.e., is a valid surrogate). Theoretical justification alone does not meet the evidentiary standards for market access.
rules.exceptions: FDA is able to rely on less well-established surrogates for accelerated approval of drugs providing meaningful benefit over existing therapies for serious or life-threatening illnesses

rules.rule_text: Dose-response studies can serve as one type of adequate and well-controlled trial contributing to substantial evidence of effectiveness when appropriate clinical endpoints or accepted surrogates are measured in appropriate populations.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1 – Contributing to Primary Evidence of Effectiveness and/or Safety
rules.verbatim_quote: A dose-response study is one kind of adequate and well-controlled trial that can provide primary clinical evidence of effectiveness... Any dose-response study includes several comparisons (e.g., each dose vs. placebo, each dose vs. lower doses). A consistent ordering of these responses... represents at least internal (within-study) replication.

rules.rule_text: When randomization to exposure (dose or concentration) is critical for an exposure-response study to support regulatory decisions by providing evidence of efficacy, such randomization must be implemented.
rules.source_section: Section V – Designs of Exposure-Response Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: When an exposure-response study is designed to support regulatory decisions by providing evidence of efficacy, randomization to exposure (dose or concentration) is critical.
rules.exceptions: Nonrandomized approaches may be used for exploratory purposes during drug discovery/development

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response studies must include measurement of all active moieties (parent and active metabolites) that contribute significantly to the effects of the drug, particularly when the route of administration is changed.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.a – Active moieties
rules.verbatim_quote: To the extent possible, it is important that exposure-response studies include measurement of all active moieties (parent and active metabolites) that contribute significantly to the effects of the drug. This is especially important when the route of administration of a drug is changed, as different routes of administration can result in different proportions of parent compound and metabolites in plasma.
rules.exceptions: For complex mixtures, measurement of major active moieties can serve as a marker of exposure

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response studies must account for protein binding by characterizing whether binding is concentration-dependent, particularly when comparing responses in patient groups with different plasma protein binding such as those with hepatic and renal disease.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.e – Unbound drug and/or active metabolite (protein binding)
rules.verbatim_quote: Where feasible, studies to determine the extent of protein binding and to understand whether this binding is or is not concentration-dependent are important, particularly when comparing responses in patient groups that can exhibit different plasma protein binding (e.g., in various stages of hepatic and renal disease).

rules.rule_text: For highly protein-bound drugs, PK and PK-PD modeling based on unbound drug concentrations may be more informative, particularly if there is significant variation in binding among patients or in special populations.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.e – Unbound drug and/or active metabolite (protein binding)
rules.verbatim_quote: For highly protein bound drugs, PK and PK-PD modeling based on unbound drug concentrations may be more informative, particularly if there is significant variation in binding among patients or in special populations of patients.

rules.rule_text: There must be a well-designed sampling plan for estimating peak plasma concentrations (Cmax) that can account for expected differences in PK profiles due to demographics, disease states, and food effects.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.b – Peak plasma concentrations (Cmax)
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important to have a well-designed sampling plan for estimating peak concentrations and be able to account for expected differences in PK profiles (e.g., in Tmax, time to Cmax) due to demographics, disease states, and food effects, if any.

rules.rule_text: For sparse plasma concentration sampling in pediatric and geriatric populations, population PK analysis combined with Bayesian estimation must be used to approximate population and individual PK parameters.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.d – Sparse plasma concentrations
rules.verbatim_quote: In these circumstances, a specialized technique, population PK analysis combined with Bayesian estimation method, can be used to approximate population and individual PK parameters, providing an exposure variable that is more readily correlated to response than the sparse plasma concentrations themselves. This approach is particularly useful when relatively complete PK information is desired, but it is difficult or unethical to sample repeatedly C for example, in pediatric and geriatric populations.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response information must be used to support new target populations (e.g., pediatric) by showing similar or altered concentration-response relationships for a well-understood short-term clinical or pharmacodynamic endpoint.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.a – New target populations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response information can sometimes be used to support use, without further clinical data, of a drug in a new target patient population by showing similar (or altered in a defined way) concentration-response relationships for a well-understood (i.e., the shape of the exposure-response curve is known), short-term clinical or pharmacodynamic endpoint.
rules.exceptions: If there is a change in the mix of parent and active metabolites, additional exposure-response data with short-term endpoints are needed

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response information linking dose, concentration, and response must be used to support dosage adjustments in patients where pharmacokinetic differences are expected due to intrinsic or extrinsic factors.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.b – Adjustment of dosages and dosing regimens in subpopulations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response information linking dose, concentration, and response can support dosage adjustments in patients where pharmacokinetic differences are expected or observed to occur because of one or more intrinsic (e.g., demographic, underlying or accompanying disease, genetic polymorphism) or extrinsic (e.g., diet, smoking, drug interactions) factors.
rules.exceptions: Exposure-response information can also support an argument that PK changes would be too small to affect response, and therefore no adjustment is needed

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response data are not likely to be useful in lieu of clinical data in supporting new dosing schedules unless the relationship of the measured responses to relevant safety and efficacy outcomes is well understood.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c – New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response data are not likely to be useful in lieu of clinical data in supporting new dosing schedules unless the relationship of the measured responses to relevant safety and efficacy outcomes is well understood.

rules.rule_text: For biological drugs, exposure-response data are not likely to obviate the need for clinical data when formulation or manufacturing changes result in altered pharmacokinetics, unless the relationships between measured responses and relevant clinical outcomes are well understood.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c – New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response data are not likely to obviate the need for clinical data when formulation or manufacturing changes result in altered pharmacokinetics, unless the relationships between measured responses and relevant clinical outcomes are well understood.

rules.rule_text: It is possible to demonstrate that differences in exposure are real but not clinically important even when the 90% confidence interval for bioequivalence measures falls within the standard 80-125%; exposure-response data can be used to support this argument.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c – New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations
rules.verbatim_quote: It is possible to demonstrate differences in exposure that are real but not clinically important, even when the 90% confidence interval for the bioequivalence measures fall within the standard of 80-125%... Rather than reformulating the product or repeating the bioequivalence study, a sponsor may be able to support the view that use of a wider confidence interval or accepting a real difference in bioavailability or exposure would not lead to a therapeutic difference.

rules.rule_text: In crossover exposure-response studies, sequence and duration of dosing must be taken into account as well as the possibility of sequence and carryover effects.
rules.source_section: Section V.A – Population vs. Individual Exposure-Response
rules.verbatim_quote: In such crossover studies, it is important to take sequence and duration of dosing into account, as well as the possibility of sequence and carryover effects.

rules.rule_text: When examining the entire drug development database for potentially interesting exposure-response relationships, sex differences in response that may be explained by observed sex-related PK data must be examined.
rules.source_section: Section V.B – Exposure-Response Study Design
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, as emphasized in the ICH E4 guidance, it is important to examine the entire drug development database for potentially interesting exposure-response relationships. For example, sex differences in response can sometimes be explained by observed sex-related PK data obtained during trials (population PK data) or in studies obtaining blood samples for measuring plasma concentrations in patients with adverse effects.

rules.rule_text: In PK-PD modeling, the following items must be described prospectively: statement of the problem, statement of assumptions, selection of the model, and validation of the model.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B – Modeling Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: In the process of PK-PD modeling, it is important to describe the following prospectively: [1. Statement of the Problem; 2. Statement of Assumptions; 3. Selection of the Model; 4. Validation of the Model]

rules.rule_text: Model assumptions must be stated and can be related to dose-response, PK, PD, and other factors including mechanism of drug actions, immediate or cumulative clinical effects, development of tolerance, drug-induced inhibition or induction of PK processes, disease state progression, and response in a placebo group.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B.2 – Statement of Assumptions
rules.verbatim_quote: The assumptions of the model that can be related to dose-response, PK, PD, and/or one or more of the following: The mechanism of the drug actions for efficacy and adverse effects; Immediate or cumulative clinical effects; Development of tolerance or absence of tolerance; Drug-induced inhibition or induction of PK processes; Disease state progression; Response in a placebo group

rules.rule_text: The final model selection must be based on the simplest model possible that has reasonable goodness of fit and provides a level of predictability appropriate for its use in decision making.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B.3 – Selection of the Model
rules.verbatim_quote: The final selection of the model will usually be based on the simplest model possible that has reasonable goodness of fit, and that provides a level of predictability appropriate for its use in decision making.

rules.rule_text: When a model is intended to provide supportive evidence for primary efficacy studies, address safety issues, or support new doses in new target populations, predictive power must be estimated, including through simulation.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B.4 – Validation of the Model
rules.verbatim_quote: The predictability is especially important when the model is intended to (1) provide supportive evidence for primary efficacy studies, (2) address safety issues, or (3) support new doses and dosing regimens in new target populations or subpopulations defined by intrinsic and extrinsic factors or when there is a change in dosage form and/or route of administration.

rules.rule_text: The exposure-response study report must be based on the ICH E3 format and must include measurements of exposure and planned or actual modeling and simulation, including assay methods, quality control samples, standard curves, and assay validity.
rules.source_section: Section VII – Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: It is advisable for the general format and content of a clinical study report to be based on that presented in the ICH E3 guidance on the Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports, modified to include measurements of exposure and planned or actual modeling and simulation. It is helpful to include a description of the assay methods used in quantifying drug concentrations (if they are components of the exposure measure) as well as assay performance (quality control samples), sample chromatograms, standard curves used, where applicable, and a description of the validity of the methodologies.

rules.rule_text: The data analysis method used in the exposure-response study must describe: the chosen dose-response or PK-PD model, the assumptions and underlying rationale for model components, the chosen model-fitting method, treatment of outliers and missing data, and representative control/command files for each significant model building step.
rules.source_section: Section VII – Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: The following components of the data analysis method used in the study would also ordinarily be described: (1) the chosen dose-response or PK-PD model, (2) the assumptions and underlying rationale for model components (e.g., parameterization, error models), (3) the chosen model-fitting method, (4) a description of the treatment of outliers and missing data, where applicable, and (5) diagrams, if possible, of the analysis performed and representative control/command files for each significant model building and/or reduction step.

rules.rule_text: The original study protocol and amendments must be included in the appendix of the exposure-response study report.
rules.source_section: Section VII – Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: Whether the analysis was performed as a result of an add-on to a clinical study or as a stand-alone exposure-response study, it is important that the original study protocol and amendments be included in the appendix.

rules.rule_text: Reports and data files for exposure-response studies must be submitted with BLA or NDA submissions in electronic format.
rules.source_section: Section VII – Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: In the meantime, sponsors are encouraged to submit both the reports and data files with BLA or NDA submissions in electronic format.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response information can represent a well-controlled clinical study contributing to substantial evidence of effectiveness where clinical endpoints or accepted surrogates are studied; this role requires the study to meet the standards of 21 CFR 314.126.
rules.source_section: Section III.B – Information to Support a Determination of Safety and Efficacy
rules.verbatim_quote: Represent a well-controlled clinical study, in some cases a particularly persuasive one, contributing to substantial evidence of effectiveness (where clinical endpoints or accepted surrogates are studied)... In general, the more critical a role that exposure-response information is to play in the establishment of efficacy, the more critical it is that it be derived from an adequate and well-controlled study (see 21 CFR 314.126).

rules.rule_text: Clinical benefit endpoints (variables that reflect how a patient feels, functions, or survives) are the most credible response measurements in clinical trials and must be used where applicable.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.3 – Clinical Benefit or Outcome Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical benefit endpoints are variables that reflect how a patient feels, functions, or survives. Clinical endpoints reflect desired effects of a therapeutic intervention and are the most credible response measurements in clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Dose-response information from ICH E4 guidance must be considered alongside this FDA guidance; dose-response data must help identify an appropriate starting dose and determine the best way to adjust dosage for a particular patient.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A – Dose and Concentration-Time Relationships
rules.verbatim_quote: As noted in the ICH E4 guidance for industry on Dose-Response Information to Support Drug Registration, dose-response information can help identify an appropriate starting dose and determine the best way (how often and by how much) to adjust dosage for a particular patient.

rules.rule_text: For drugs with a significant first-kind (nonrandomized) concentration-response study, the analysis must use specialized approaches to address potential confounding; the potential problem of interrelated factors leading to both an effect on PK and an effect on response must be addressed.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B – Concentration-Response Relationships: Two Approaches
rules.verbatim_quote: The first kind of study (# 1 above) is the usual or most common way of obtaining exposure-response information, but this kind of study can be misleading unless it is analyzed using specialized approaches (e.g., Sheiner, Hashimoto, and Beal 1991).

rules.rule_text: Measurement of response endpoints must be standardized across studies and between study sites and/or laboratories to establish valid exposure-response relationships.
rules.source_section: Section V.D – Measuring Response
rules.verbatim_quote: In most cases, it is important to standardize the measurement of response endpoints across studies and between study sites and/or laboratories.

rules.rule_text: For PK-PD studies assessing pediatric populations, a decision tree approach integrating similarity of disease progression, similarity of response to intervention, and available PD measurements must be used to determine the extent of required PK and PD studies.
rules.source_section: Appendix B – Pediatric Decision Tree Integration of PK-PD
rules.verbatim_quote: [Pediatric Study Decision Tree] Reasonable to assume (pediatrics vs adults): similar disease progression? similar response to intervention? ... If YES TO BOTH: Reasonable to assume similar concentration-response (C-R) in pediatrics and adults? If YES: Conduct PK studies to achieve levels similar to adults; Conduct safety trials.
